critic_raw:
The claim that the framework 'performs well across strategic and non-strategic users' requires testing on populations with known mixtures of user types. If the evaluation is conducted on datasets where all users are known to be strategic (e.g., credit fraud data where all labeled fraudsters gamed the system) or all non-strategic, performance on mixed populations is entirely untested.

End-to-end training through strategic user responses requires a differentiable model of user best-response behavior. The specific best-response model assumed (e.g., myopic best-response, best-response with cost, Stackelberg equilibrium) determines what manipulation strategies the classifier learns to resist. An incorrect best-response model means the classifier is robust to a type of strategic behavior that doesn't match real users.

The social outcome regularization encodes a fixed definition of 'favorable outcomes' at training time. In strategic classification settings, users adapt to the classifier, which changes the distribution of manipulation strategies over time. A regularization objective that was aligned with social good at training time may enforce an outdated objective as user behavior evolves — the methodology has no mechanism for updating the social objective.
all_issues_raised: The claim of robustness to both strategic and non-strategic users cannot be validated without evaluation on populations with known mixtures of user types; single-type evaluation datasets do not support the mixed-population claim.

The differentiable best-response model assumed for end-to-end training is not specified; misspecified user rationality (myopic vs. Stackelberg vs. cost-constrained) means the classifier resists a type of manipulation that doesn't match real users.
The social outcome regularization encodes a fixed training-time objective that cannot adapt as user strategic behavior evolves post-deployment.
all_issues_adjudicated: The claim of robustness to both strategic and non-strategic users cannot be validated without evaluation on populations with known mixtures of user types; single-type evaluation datasets do not support the mixed-population claim.

The differentiable best-response model assumed for end-to-end training is not specified; misspecified user rationality (myopic vs. Stackelberg vs. cost-constrained) means the classifier resists a type of manipulation that doesn't match real users.
The social outcome regularization encodes a fixed training-time objective that cannot adapt as user strategic behavior evolves post-deployment.
